title: ML & AI Fundamentals for Staff+
description: Every FAANG Staff+ engineer is expected to understand ML fundamentals — not to build models but to make architectural decisions about AI integration, RAG design, and ML system architecture at enterprise scale.

sections.title: ML Pipeline Basics

description.value:
An ML pipeline is the end-to-end system for building, deploying, and maintaining machine learning models at scale. FAANG Staff+ engineers must understand the full pipeline — not just model training, but data ingestion, feature engineering, model validation, deployment, monitoring, and retraining. The pipeline is a data engineering problem as much as a modeling problem.

Data Ingestion & Processing: Raw data comes from multiple sources: logs, databases, streams (Kafka), and batch files. The data must be validated (schema checks, null detection, outlier identification), cleaned, and transformed into a format suitable for feature engineering. At FAANG scale, this means petabyte-scale data pipelines using Spark, Flink, or Beam. The SAP equivalent is BW data extraction: ETL processes extracting from ECC, transforming via DTP, and loading into InfoCubes. The concepts are identical, only the scale differs.

Feature Engineering: Features are the input variables that models learn from. Feature engineering is the most impactful step — better features beat better models. Common techniques: normalization (scaling to [0,1] or z-score), one-hot encoding (categorical → binary vector), embedding (learned dense representation for categorical/text data), and feature crossing (combining features to capture interactions). SAP CDS Views with calculated fields are a form of feature engineering — defining derived attributes that serve as inputs to business logic.

Training & Validation: The training pipeline splits data into training/validation/test sets, trains the model (linear regression, tree-based, neural network), tunes hyperparameters (learning rate, tree depth, regularization), and evaluates metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC-ROC). MLflow or Kubeflow tracks experiments, parameters, and metrics for reproducibility. FAANG teams typically run hundreds of training experiments per model to find optimal configurations.

Deployment & Serving: Models are deployed as inference endpoints (REST API via TensorFlow Serving, TorchServe, or custom containers). FAANG uses feature stores (Feast, Tecton) to serve features consistently between training and inference — eliminating training-serving skew. Model versioning and A/B testing frameworks (custom or using an ML platform) enable safe rollouts.

Monitoring & Retraining: Deployed models degrade over time due to data drift (distribution of input features changes) and concept drift (relationship between features and target changes). Monitoring detects drift, triggers retraining pipelines, and can auto-rollback to a previous model version. SAP's predictive quality and predictive maintenance follow the same monitoring + retraining cycle.

sections.codeBlock:
# ML Pipeline: Data → Feature → Train → Deploy → Monitor → Retrain

def ml_pipeline(raw_data_source, model_registry):
    # 1. Data Ingestion (SAP BW extraction equivalent)
    data = ingest(raw_data_source)  # Spark/Flink job
    validated = validate_schema(data, expected_schema)

    # 2. Feature Engineering (CDS View calculated fields)
    features = compute_features(validated)
    # Normalize, one-hot encode, handle nulls
    from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    normalized = scaler.fit_transform(features)

    # 3. Train/Validate split
    from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
    X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
        normalized, labels, test_size=0.2, random_state=42
    )

    # 4. Train model
    from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
    model = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100)
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)

    # 5. Validate (MLflow tracking)
    from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, f1_score
    preds = model.predict(X_val)
    metrics = {"accuracy": accuracy_score(y_val, preds),
               "f1": f1_score(y_val, preds, average="weighted")}
    log_metrics_to_mlflow(metrics)

    # 6. Deploy (Kubernetes + model registry)
    if metrics["f1"] > deployment_threshold:
        model_registry.register(model, version="v2", stage="production")

    # 7. Monitor + Retrain loop (drift detection)
    return model

decisionTree.title: Choosing the Right ML Approach for an Enterprise Problem
decisionTree.prompt: Should I use a pre-built ML API, fine-tune an open-source model, train a custom model, or use a rules-based approach for this enterprise AI problem?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "How specific is the task and how much labeled data do we have?" If the task is a common NLP/vision task (classification, extraction, summarization) and general accuracy is sufficient, use a pre-built API. If the task is domain-specific (SAP materials classification, purchase order extraction) with 1K-10K labeled examples, fine-tune an open-source model. If the task requires 99%+ accuracy on proprietary patterns with 100K+ examples, train a custom model. If the task has clear deterministic rules (approval routing, status transitions), use rules.

branches.condition: General NLP/vision task with standard output format — no special domain knowledge needed
branches.path: Pre-built API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google AI, AWS Bedrock). No training required. Pay-per-use. Best for: chatbots, document summarization, general classification. SAP example: using GPT-4 to summarize purchase order descriptions. Cost: $0.01-0.10 per API call. Latency: 1-5 seconds.

branches.condition: Domain-specific task with moderate labeled data — SAP materials, legal contracts, medical records
branches.path: Fine-tune open-source LLM (LLaMA, Mistral, BERT variants) on 1K-10K labeled examples. Use LoRA for efficient fine-tuning ($50-200 per training run). Best for: materials classification, PO line-item extraction, contract clause detection. SAP example: fine-tune BERT to classify 10,000 materials into SAP categories. Cost: $2-5K total (compute + labeling). Latency: 100-500ms.

branches.condition: Mission-critical, high-volume, proprietary — needs 99%+ accuracy on unique patterns
branches.path: Train custom model from scratch (or heavy fine-tuning). Requires 100K+ labeled examples. Data pipeline + feature store + training infra + MLOps. Cost: $100K-1M+. Best for: fraud detection, credit scoring, predictive maintenance. SAP example: custom model predicting equipment failure from 500K sensor readings. Not recommended for most use cases.

branches.condition: Deterministic business rules — approval workflows, status transitions, validation checks
branches.path: Rules-based approach (SAP BRFplus, decision tables, CDS view logic). No ML needed. Best for: approval routing (amount > $10K → manager approval), status transitions (Draft → Submitted → Approved), data validation (delivery date cannot be in past). SAP example: BRFplus rules for pricing and discounting.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The Staff+ answer: start with the simplest approach that meets requirements. Pre-built API first, then fine-tuning if accuracy is insufficient, then custom model only if the others fail. 80% of enterprise ML problems are solved by pre-built APIs. The other 20% need fine-tuning. Custom models from scratch are almost never justified.

sections.title: Transformer Architecture

description.value:
The Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) is the foundation of all modern LLMs — GPT, BERT, LLaMA, Claude, Gemini. FAANG Staff+ engineers must understand the architecture at a conceptual level to design AI-integrated systems, even if they don't train models. The key innovation is the self-attention mechanism, which replaces recurrent connections with parallelizable attention computation.

Self-Attention: Each token in the input sequence computes attention scores against all other tokens. For each token, three vectors are computed: Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V). The attention score is softmax(Q·K^T / sqrt(d_k)) · V. The dot product Q·K^T measures compatibility between tokens. The scaling factor sqrt(d_k) prevents gradient vanishing for large dimensions. Self-attention allows the model to capture long-range dependencies — a word at position 100 can attend directly to a word at position 1 without going through 99 intermediate steps (unlike RNNs).

Multi-Head Attention: Instead of computing one attention distribution, the model computes multiple (typically 8–128) attention distributions in parallel, each learning different relationship types (syntactic, semantic, positional). The heads are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. This is analogous to SAP's company-code-level parallel processing — multiple company codes processed independently in parallel, results merged.

Encoder-Decoder Structure: The original Transformer has an encoder (reads input sequence, produces representations) and a decoder (generates output sequence autoregressively). GPT uses only the decoder block (autoregressive language modeling). BERT uses only the encoder block (bidirectional understanding). The encoder self-attention can attend to all tokens; decoder self-attention is masked (can't attend to future tokens during generation). Cross-attention in the decoder attends to encoder outputs.

Positional Encoding: Since self-attention is permutation-invariant (no inherent notion of word order), positional encodings are added to input embeddings. Sinusoidal encodings use sine/cosine functions of different frequencies, allowing the model to learn relative positions. Modern LLMs use learned positional embeddings or rotary position embeddings (RoPE).

SAP Analogy: The attention mechanism is like a CDS view with parameterized input parameters where the query determines which columns to select based on relevance scores. Multi-head attention is like running multiple CDS views in parallel on the same data source, each with different selection criteria, then merging results. The Transformer's feed-forward network (FFN) after each attention layer is like ABAP business logic applied to the attention output — transforming selected features into higher-level representations.

# Simplified Self-Attention computation
import numpy as np

def self_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    """
    Q, K, V: (batch, seq_len, d_k)
    Returns: (batch, seq_len, d_v)
    """
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    # Attention scores: Q * K^T / sqrt(d_k)
    scores = np.matmul(Q, K.transpose(0, 2, 1)) / np.sqrt(d_k)
    
    if mask is not None:
        scores = np.where(mask, -1e9, scores)  # causal masking
    
    # Softmax normalization
    weights = np.exp(scores - np.max(scores, axis=-1, keepdims=True))
    weights /= np.sum(weights, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    
    # Weighted sum of values
    output = np.matmul(weights, V)
    return output

# Multi-Head Attention: split d_model into h heads
def multi_head_attention(Q, K, V, h=8):
    d_model = Q.shape[-1]
    d_k = d_model // h
    
    # Project and split into h heads
    heads = []
    for i in range(h):
        Q_i = Q[:, :, i*d_k:(i+1)*d_k]
        K_i = K[:, :, i*d_k:(i+1)*d_k]
        V_i = V[:, :, i*d_k:(i+1)*d_k]
        head_out = self_attention(Q_i, K_i, V_i)
        heads.append(head_out)
    
    # Concatenate heads and project
    return np.concatenate(heads, axis=-1)

# Note: In ABAP terms, this is like running multiple
# parallel CDS queries with different selection criteria
# (heads) and merging results.

sections.codeBlock:
# Self-Attention: the core Transformer mechanism
import numpy as np

def self_attention(Q, K, V, mask=None):
    # Q, K, V: (batch, seq_len, d_k)
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    # Score = Q * K^T / sqrt(d_k)
    scores = np.matmul(Q, K.transpose(0, 2, 1)) / np.sqrt(d_k)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = np.where(mask, -1e9, scores)
    # Softmax: attention weights
    weights = np.exp(scores - np.max(scores, axis=-1, keepdims=True))
    weights /= np.sum(weights, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    # Weighted sum of values
    return np.matmul(weights, V)

def multi_head_attention(Q, K, V, h=8):
    d_model = Q.shape[-1]
    d_k = d_model // h
    heads = []
    for i in range(h):
        Q_i = Q[:, :, i*d_k:(i+1)*d_k]
        K_i = K[:, :, i*d_k:(i+1)*d_k]
        V_i = V[:, :, i*d_k:(i+1)*d_k]
        heads.append(self_attention(Q_i, K_i, V_i))
    return np.concatenate(heads, axis=-1)

organizationalDimension.title: Building Enterprise LLM Literacy — Understanding Transformers for Non-ML Engineers
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: Start with a 90-minute 'Transformers for Engineers' lunch-and-learn that uses analogies your team already understands. Self-attention = CDS association join with weighted relevance. Multi-head attention = parallel company code processing. KV-cache = SAP buffer table. After the session, share a one-pager that maps every Transformer concept to an SAP/ABAP equivalent. Follow up with a hands-on session where engineers use an LLM API to build a simple RAG pipeline on SAP documentation.

stakeholders.name: ABAP Developers
stakeholders.concern: Transformers feel like advanced math (matrices, softmax, backprop) — irrelevant to daily work
stakeholders.engagement: Show how LLMs are consumed through APIs, not built. The SAP equivalent is using an API function module — you don't need to understand the implementation to use it. Focus on architectural patterns (RAG, prompt engineering) not training.

stakeholders.name: Solution Architects
stakeholders.concern: LLM integration adds complexity and cost to enterprise architecture. When is it worth it?
stakeholders.engagement: Provide decision framework: pre-built API vs fine-tuning vs custom model. Show cost-benefit analysis for common SAP AI scenarios (materials classification, PO extraction, intelligent search).

stakeholders.name: Cloud Platform Team
stakeholders.concern: LLM integration requires GPU infrastructure, vector databases, and new monitoring — operational burden
stakeholders.engagement: Plan for managed services (OpenAI API, Bedrock, Vertex AI) rather than self-hosted models. Vector DB can be pgvector on existing PostgreSQL. Start with zero-infrastructure approach (API-only).

activities: Create analogy one-pager (Transformer concepts → SAP equivalents)
Share case studies of SAP AI success stories (SAP AI Core, Joule)
Set up sandbox API keys for GPT-4 / Claude / Gemini
Build a simple RAG pipeline on SAP documentation
Hackathon: 'Build an AI feature for SAP in 2 days'
Define LLM integration patterns (RAG, fine-tuning, API proxy)
Create reference architecture for SAP + LLM
Publish security guidelines (PII handling, data leakage prevention)

metrics: Engineers who can explain Transformer architecture using SAP analogies (target: 80%)
Number of SAP + LLM prototypes built (target: 5+ in 3 months)
LLM API cost per team per month (target: < $500/team during exploration phase)
antiPatterns: Requiring ML PhD to integrate LLMs — modern LLMs are consumed via HTTP APIs; no ML expertise needed
Self-hosting LLMs without justification — API costs $0.01-0.10/call; self-hosting costs $5-20/hour for GPU
Ignoring data privacy — SAP data contains PII, financials, trade secrets. Never send to public LLM APIs without data masking or using private deployments

sections.title: RAG Design Patterns

description.value:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the dominant pattern for enterprise LLM applications. Instead of fine-tuning a model on proprietary data (expensive, hard to update), RAG retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base and provides them as context to the LLM for generation. This is the architecture behind ChatGPT plugins, Bing Copilot, and most enterprise AI implementations.

Indexing Pipeline: Documents are ingested, chunked into segments (typically 256–1024 tokens), embedded into vector representations using an embedding model (e.g., text-embedding-3-small, BGE, E5), and stored in a vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus, pgvector). Each chunk is indexed with its embedding vector and metadata (source, date, page). The chunk size is a critical tradeoff: smaller chunks increase retrieval precision but lose context.

Retrieval Pipeline: When a user query arrives, it's embedded using the same embedding model. The vector database performs approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search to find the top-K most similar chunks (typically K=3–10). HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is the most common ANN algorithm, offering O(log n) search time with high recall. The retrieved chunks are ranked and optionally reranked by a cross-encoder model for improved precision.

Generation Pipeline: The retrieved chunks are inserted into a prompt template alongside the user's question. The prompt instructs the LLM to answer based solely on the provided context (reducing hallucination). The LLM generates the response, which may include citations to source chunks. Advanced RAG patterns include: HyDE (generate hypothetical document before retrieval), multi-hop RAG (multiple retrieval steps for complex reasoning), and agentic RAG (LLM decides when and what to retrieve).

Reranking: The initial ANN retrieval may surface irrelevant chunks. A cross-encoder reranker (e.g., Cohere rerank, BGE-reranker) scores each chunk against the query jointly (not just embedding similarity), improving relevance. Reranking is typically applied to the top 50–100 chunks before selecting the final K=3–10.

SAP Analogy: RAG is exactly what SAP does with its Enterprise Search / TREX engine. Documents are indexed (indexing pipeline), user queries are processed against the search index (retrieval), and search results are displayed with context (generation). SAP's Knowledge Warehouse and SAP Enable Now follow the same index-retrieve-present pattern. The FAANG RAG stack is simply a more sophisticated version with learned representations (embeddings) instead of TF-IDF/BM25 term matching.

# RAG Pipeline pseudocode
from typing import List
import numpy as np

class RAGPipeline:
    def __init__(self, embedding_model, llm, vector_db):
        self.embedder = embedding_model  # e.g., text-embedding-3-small
        self.llm = llm                   # e.g., GPT-4
        self.vector_db = vector_db       # e.g., Pinecone index

    def index_documents(self, documents: List[str]):
        """Indexing pipeline: chunk → embed → store"""
        chunks = self._chunk_documents(documents, chunk_size=512)
        for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
            embedding = self.embedder.encode(chunk)
            self.vector_db.upsert([(str(i), embedding, {"text": chunk})])

    def query(self, user_query: str, k: int = 5) -> str:
        """Retrieval + Generation pipeline"""
        # Step 1: Embed the query
        query_embedding = self.embedder.encode(user_query)

        # Step 2: ANN search in vector DB
        results = self.vector_db.query(
            query_embedding, top_k=k * 2
        )  # fetch 2x for reranking

        # Step 3: Rerank with cross-encoder
        reranked = self._rerank(user_query, results)[:k]

        # Step 4: Build prompt with context
        context = "\n---\n".join([r["text"] for r in reranked])
        prompt = f"""Answer the question based ONLY on the context below.
If the context doesn't contain the answer, say "I don't know."

Context:\n{context}\n
Question: {user_query}\n
Answer:"""

        # Step 5: Generate response
        return self.llm.generate(prompt)

    def _rerank(self, query, results):
        # Cross-encoder: score each (query, doc) pair
        scored = []
        for r in results:
            score = self.cross_encoder.predict(query, r["text"])
            scored.append((score, r))
        scored.sort(reverse=True)
        return [r for _, r in scored]

sections.codeBlock:
# RAG Pipeline: Retrieve → Augment → Generate
from typing import List

class RAGPipeline:
    def __init__(self, embedder, llm, vector_db):
        self.embedder = embedder
        self.llm = llm
        self.vector_db = vector_db

    def index_documents(self, documents: List[str]):
        chunks = self._chunk(documents, chunk_size=512)
        for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
            embedding = self.embedder.encode(chunk)
            self.vector_db.upsert([(str(i), embedding, {"text": chunk})])

    def query(self, user_query: str, k: int = 5) -> str:
        # Step 1: Embed query
        q_emb = self.embedder.encode(user_query)
        # Step 2: ANN search
        results = self.vector_db.query(q_emb, top_k=k * 2)
        # Step 3: Rerank with cross-encoder
        reranked = self._rerank(user_query, results)[:k]
        # Step 4: Build prompt with context
        context = "\n---\n".join([r["text"] for r in reranked])
        prompt = f"Answer based on context.\nContext:\n{context}\n\nQ: {user_query}"
        # Step 5: Generate
        return self.llm.generate(prompt)

    def _rerank(self, query, results):
        scored = [(self.cross_encoder.predict(query, r["text"]), r) for r in results]
        scored.sort(reverse=True)
        return [r for _, r in scored]

adversarialResponse.title: When RAG Hallucinates Critical SAP Data — The CFO's Audit Problem
adversarialResponse.challenge: You've built a RAG-powered SAP support assistant that answers questions about financial close procedures by retrieving from your internal SAP documentation. During a dry-run, the CFO asks: 'What is the journal entry reversal process for Q4 adjustments?' The RAG system retrieves a section about 'Q3 adjustment reversals' and generates a plausible-sounding answer that incorrectly describes the Q4 process (which has additional approval steps). The CFO nearly follows the wrong procedure. If this had gone unnoticed, it would have caused a material misstatement caught by external auditors.
adversarialResponse.badResponse: Disable the RAG assistant entirely. 'LLMs are too risky for financial data.' The team rebuilds a traditional search interface (Elasticsearch + keyword matching). Users hate it — it returns 47 documents with no synthesis. The project is shelved, and the $500K investment is written off. The company falls behind competitors who successfully deploy AI assistants.
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: Implement a multi-layered hallucination mitigation system: (1) Add citation requirement — every statement in the generated answer must cite its source chunk. If the LLM can't cite a source, it must say 'I don't know.' (2) Implement a 'factuality checker' — a secondary LLM call that verifies each claim against the source chunks and flags unverifiable claims. (3) Add a confidence threshold — if the average similarity score of retrieved chunks is below 0.75, surface a disclaimer: 'This answer is based on partial documentation. Verify with the Finance team.' (4) For critical domains (financial close, compliance), add a human-in-the-loop step: the assistant drafts the answer, but a human must approve before it's sent to the user. Result: hallucination rate drops from 15% to 0.5%. CFO approves the assistant for 'informational use with verification.' Six months later, after 10,000 verified answers with zero errors, the CFO removes the human-in-the-loop requirement for low-risk domains.
adversarialResponse.pattern: Defense-in-depth for LLM applications: Citation requirement + factuality checker + confidence threshold + human-in-the-loop for critical domains. This is the same pattern as SAP's own AI guardrails in SAP AI Core and Joule. Never deploy an LLM application in enterprise without at least two layers of hallucination mitigation.

sections.title: ML System Design at Scale

description.value:
FAANG Staff+ engineers must design ML systems that operate at internet scale. This is not about building models — it's about building the infrastructure around models: feature stores, training infrastructure, serving infrastructure, monitoring, and the feedback loop. These are distributed systems engineering problems with ML-specific constraints.

Feature Stores (Feast / Tecton): A feature store is a centralized repository for feature definitions, computation, and serving. It ensures that features used during training are identical to features used during inference — eliminating training-serving skew, the #1 cause of production ML failures. Features are either batch-computed (daily/hourly from data lakes) or stream-computed (real-time from Kafka). The feature store provides point-in-time correct feature retrieval (e.g., "what was the 7-day average for this user at the time of prediction?"). SAP equivalent: CDS Views act as a feature store — defined once, used in both reporting (training) and operational transactions (inference). The CDS view ensures the same logic in both contexts.

Training-Serving Skew: The silent killer of ML systems. Skew occurs when the data distribution differs between training and serving. Causes: different feature computation logic, different data sources, different timestamps, missing values handled differently, or embedding model versions diverging. Mitigations: feature store (single source of truth), online evaluation (shadow traffic), canary deployments, and automated skew detection (comparing training vs serving feature distributions using KS-test or PSI).

Real-Time Inference: Low-latency model serving (P99 < 10ms). Requires optimized inference engines (TensorRT, ONNX Runtime, Triton Inference Server), model quantization (FP32 → FP16/INT8), and batching (dynamic batching groups requests for GPU efficiency). For LLM inference: KV-cache optimization, continuous batching (vLLM), speculative decoding (draft model + target model). Real-time feature computation uses Flink/Kafka Streams with sliding window aggregations. SAP equivalent: real-time pricing and credit scoring in SAP S/4HANA uses the same patterns — precomputed factors + real-time adjustments with caching.

ML System Anti-Patterns: (1) Training and serving using different code paths (always use the same feature logic). (2) Not logging predictions and actuals (you can't detect drift without ground truth). (3) Manual model deployment without canary/rollback (models are code — they need CI/CD). (4) Ignoring data quality (garbage in, garbage out — monitor input distributions continuously). (5) Single-point inference (use ensemble/shadow models for fallback).

SAP AI Core & Joule: SAP's AI platform embeds ML into enterprise processes. Joule is the copilot experience. Staff+ engineers designing integrations with SAP AI Core must understand how to invoke ML pipelines via APIs, manage model versions, handle training data from SAP tables, and serve predictions back into SAP workflows — exactly the same architecture as any FAANG ML platform, just at enterprise scale with different SLAs.

# Feature Store: Training vs Serving consistency
class FeatureStore:
    """
    Centralized feature computation.
    Same code used for training (batch) and inference (online).
    """
    def __init__(self, online_store, offline_store):
        self.online = online_store   # Redis/DynamoDB for real-time
        self.offline = offline_store  # BigQuery/S3 for training

    def get_features(self, entity_key, feature_names, point_in_time=None):
        """
        Point-in-time correct feature retrieval.
        Used during training to avoid future data leakage.
        """
        if point_in_time:
            return self.offline.query_as_of(
                entity_key, feature_names, point_in_time
            )
        return self.online.get(entity_key, feature_names)

    def compute_features(self, raw_data):
        """
        Single implementation of feature computation.
        Called identically in batch (Spark) and online (Flink).
        """
        features = {}
        # 7-day rolling average (sliding window)
        features["avg_7d"] = raw_data.rolling(7).mean()
        # Z-score normalization
        features["z_score"] = (raw_data - raw_data.mean()) / raw_data.std()
        return features

# Data drift monitoring
def detect_drift(training_dist, serving_dist, threshold=0.05):
    from scipy.stats import ks_2samp
    stat, p_value = ks_2samp(training_dist, serving_dist)
    if p_value < threshold:
        alert("Significant data drift detected!")
        trigger_retraining()
    return stat, p_value

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Batch Inference vs Real-Time ML Serving for Enterprise AI

variables.name: C_batch_infra
variables.description: Monthly batch inference infrastructure cost (Spark cluster, storage)

variables.name: C_real_infra
variables.description: Monthly real-time inference infra cost (GPU serving, Redis, load balancer)

variables.name: L_batch
variables.description: Batch inference latency (hours) — time from data available to prediction ready

variables.name: V_freshness
variables.description: Value of data freshness per hour (how much better is a real-time prediction vs 4-hour-old prediction)

variables.name: C_retrain_batch
variables.description: Monthly cost to retrain model on batch schedule (once/week)

variables.name: C_retrain_real
variables.description: Monthly cost to retrain model on continuous schedule (online learning)

calculation.formula: Batch: total = C_batch_infra + C_retrain_batch − (value_of_latency_acceptable). Real-time: total = C_real_infra + C_retrain_real + (value_of_latency_premium).
calculation.cp: Batch: $8,000 + $3,000 = $11,000/month. Data is 4 hours stale. Value: 500K × $0.02 × 4h = $40,000 lost freshness/day = $1.2M/month. Effective cost: $1,211,000/month.
calculation.ap: Real-time: $25,000 + $12,000 = $37,000/month. Data is 50ms fresh. Value: 500K × $0.02 × 0.0000139h = negligible loss. Effective cost: $37,000/month.
calculation.breakeven: Real-time ML is cheaper by $1.17M/month when data freshness has monetary value. Batch only wins if: (1) predictions don't benefit from freshness (e.g., daily credit risk scoring), (2) prediction volume is low (< 10K/day), or (3) the use case is analytical (dashboards, reports) not operational (real-time decisions). For fraud detection, recommendation systems, and dynamic pricing — real-time is mandatory.

details: Why ABAP engineers need ML: SAP has embedded AI (SAP AI Core, Joule). Staff+ candidates must discuss ML integration — building intelligent automation, embedding LLMs into enterprise workflows, and designing data pipelines for ML training at scale. FAANG expects architectural understanding of ML systems even from non-ML roles.
